Stage 02

Sandbox Discovery

Run a small set of approved experiments without sensitive data, and capture what you learn somewhere leadership can actually see it.

The Sandbox pact

1. Nothing made in the sandbox gets published.

The purpose of the sandbox is learning, not production. Code written here, models trained here, and prompts refined here are strictly prototypes. This absolute boundary gives the team permission to fail fast and experiment widely without the risk of accidental deployment.

2. Private information stays private — by process, not by promise.

No PII, no sensitive client data, no unreleased financials enter the sandbox environment. We rely on strict data sanitization protocols and mock datasets, ensuring that even if an experiment goes awry, organizational integrity remains uncompromised.

How the experiment runs

8-Week Timeline

Step 01

Recipes

Identify and document specific, testable use cases. These "recipes" outline the precise inputs, expected outputs, and the business hypothesis being tested.

Step 02

Experiment

Execute the recipes within the isolated sandbox environment. Focus on speed and iteration, adjusting prompts and parameters to find the edges of the capability.

Step 03

Log the value

Quantify the results. Did it save time? Did it improve quality? Document the tangible outcomes of the experiment to build a case for broader adoption.

Step 04

Open visibility

Share the findings openly across the organization. Transparency prevents duplicated effort and sparks new ideas in adjacent teams.

Step 05

Governance review

Formal evaluation of the experiment's outcomes against organizational risk and strategic alignment.

  • Green: Approved for wider pilot or restricted production use.
  • Yellow: Promising, but requires further refinement or risk mitigation.
  • Red: Unviable due to cost, risk, or lack of value. Terminate experiment.

"What you walk away with is a recipe book your organization actually agreed on."

Required before proceeding

  • check_circle Clear definition of success metrics for each recipe.
  • check_circle Approval from InfoSec on the isolated sandbox environment.
  • check_circle Commitment from leadership to review the findings openly.

If you skip this stage

Bypassing the sandbox invites immediate technical debt and unacceptable risk. Implementing untested "recipes" in production environments often leads to data leaks, hallucinated outputs affecting client deliverables, and a rapid erosion of trust in the technology across the organization. You will build solutions nobody agreed upon.
